text: The surgeon demonstrates the repair of an umbilical hernia, placing a mesh in the preperitoneal space to reinforce the abdominal wall.
The surgeon reduces the hernia contents, carefully manipulating and repositioning them back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon makes an incision in the peritoneum proximal to the camera view, accessing the preperitoneal space for mesh placement during the umbilical hernia repair.
The surgeon continues to take down the preperitoneum closest to the camera, revealing the hernia defect clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects up into the fascia, revealing the hernia sac at the base of the umbilicus.
The surgeon continues to encircle the hernia sac, gradually reducing it within the surgical field.
In the case of large hernias, the surgeon may need to transect and resect the hernia sac from the outside, visually separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon amputates the hernia sac from the base of the umbilicus, using posterior retraction to facilitate the removal.
The surgeon inverts the hernia sac, visually folding it inward to facilitate its reduction within the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the distal dissection laterally, carefully freeing the peritoneum from the posterior rectal sheath.
The surgeon prepares to place a physio mesh of determined size into the surgical site, following the dissection and preparation of the hernia area.
The surgeon closes the hernia defect using a zero PDS strata fixed barb suture in a running fashion, securing the tissue edges together.
The surgeon places the physio mesh into the dissected space after closing the hernia defect, ensuring proper positioning within the surgical field.
The surgeon places the physio mesh into the dissected space without fixation sutures, with the option to use permanent sutures to attach the mesh to the posterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon uses a IV-O monocryl Stratafix barbed suture to close the peritoneum, effectively excluding the physio mesh from the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon overlaps the barb suture to lock it in place, securing the closure of the hernia repair.
The completed hernia repair shows the mesh placed in the preperitoneal space, effectively separating it from the bowel.
